title: Software for an Audience of One
description: Part 5 of a 6-part series on AI-native computing. The marginal cost of bespoke software collapsed to near zero, inverting the economics that forced fifty years of mass software. Code becomes disposable; intent and data are the assets. Companion video to the essay of the same name.

beats.text: Software for an audience of one. This is part five of a six part series about AI-native computing — the computer that understands, and the computer that builds. This part is about money. About what happens when building software stops being expensive.

eyebrow.label: Part 5 of 6 · Beyond the Sea of App Icons

slide.title: Software for an **Audience of One**
slide.subtitle: What happens when building software becomes {primary:nearly free}
slide.author: Satoshi Nakajima

beats.text: Here is a real thing. A restaurant guide, drawn as a Tokyo subway map. The lines are neighborhoods. The stations are restaurants. It glows on one person's home screen, and it was made for exactly one person — its owner.
beats.imagePrompt: Editorial illustration on a deep navy blue background with glowing cyan and violet accents. A large tablet screen glowing warmly in a dim, cozy room at night, displaying a stylized Tokyo subway map: colorful luminous transit lines in cyan, violet, and warm amber connecting small round restaurant markers — tiny bowls of ramen, teacups, and paper lanterns as the stations. The map feels personal and hand-tailored, like a private treasure belonging to one person. Painterly, cinematic lighting, warm accents against deep navy, no text, no logos.

beats.text: That map exists today. So does a flashcard drill for one person's vocabulary. And a poster wall for one person's watchlist. Each one is a small piece of software an agent wrote on request. The owner never saw a line of the code. He described what he wanted — and the software appeared.

slide.title: Software with an audience of exactly one

items.title: Subway map
items.description: one person's restaurants, as transit lines

items.title: Flashcards
items.description: a drill for one person's vocabulary

slide.footer: Three of six views in one real workspace. The owner never saw a line of the code.

beats.text: No company would ever build any of these. Maggie Appleton gave the category its exact name: software no industrial software company would build, because there is not enough market value in it. The market for that subway map is one person. For fifty years, that made it impossible. Not hard — impossible.

slide.quote: “Software that no industrial software company would build — there's not enough market value in doing it.”
slide.author: Maggie Appleton · Local-First conference, 2024
slide.role: The addressable market is exactly one person

beats.text: Why impossible? Arithmetic. Building software was expensive — teams, years, paid up front. But serving one more user cost nothing. So the rational move was to spread that big fixed cost over as many users as possible. Build for the mass. And everyone gets the average.

slide.title: Why software was built for the mass

callout.text: The workflow that's 80% right and forever 20% wrong — not bad taste, just {warning:the arithmetic.}
callout.label: The symptom

beats.text: Now remove one term. A model that writes working code on demand makes building nearly free. And the whole structure falls. If building for one person costs the same as building for millions, the reason to build for millions disappears. You build for exactly this person.

slide.title: One term removed — the structure falls

content.label: of users to repay a build

content.value: Building is expensive → aim at the mass.
Building is nearly free → aim at this person.

beats.text: So a fifty-year-old rule flips. The code is disposable. Intent and data are the asset.

slide.quote: The code is {warning:disposable}; intent and data are {primary:the asset.}
slide.author: Software for an Audience of One

beats.text: Look at where the value actually sits. The intent is durable — draw my restaurants as a subway map. The data is durable — the restaurants themselves. The code in between is just a middle step, regrown on demand. Also color the stations by cuisine? That opens no code review. It reissues the request.

slide.title: The durable things are the two ends

content.value: *“Draw my restaurants as a subway map.”*

callout.text: “Also color the stations by cuisine” opens no code review — it {primary:reissues the request.}
callout.label: Maintenance

beats.text: So nobody maintains this software. When it stops fitting, you say the sentence again — and a new version simply replaces the old one. It is cheaper to regrow the artifact than to repair it.
beats.imagePrompt: Editorial illustration on a deep navy blue background with cyan and violet glow. A glowing subway-map artifact caught mid-transformation: on the left, the old version dissolving into drifting particles of light; on the right, a fresh brighter version of the same map crystallizing into place, its luminous lines rerouting and its station markers recoloring, all fed by a soft stream of light suggesting a spoken sentence. Warm amber accents, painterly, cinematic, elegant, no text, no logos.

beats.text: And no — this is not brand new. The spreadsheet got here first. A budget model in Excel is software for an audience of one. But the spreadsheet made you the programmer. You built it yourself, cell by cell, inside a grid. What's new is a double removal: the surface becomes arbitrary code, and the agent does the building. That double removal is the second pillar — the computer that builds — arriving for people who will never read code.

slide.title: The spreadsheet got here first — at a price

left.title: Spreadsheet · no-code

items: Inside a grid or block palette

left.footer: Bespoke — but you became the programmer

items: Any surface — even a subway map

callout.text: The {highlight:double removal} is the actual novelty — both constraints gone at once.
callout.label: What's new

beats.text: Now the honest part. This gives something up. Mass software is debugged by millions of people — every bug you never hit, someone else already found. Software for one has no such crowd. Its bugs ship to their only user. You. The spreadsheet taught this lesson too: the danger was never the visible screen. It was the invisible formula error nobody else ever checked.

right.title: Software for one

content.value: The bug ships to **its only user: you.**

right.footer: No other eyes will ever run it

callout.text: The wrong poster wall is harmless. The wrong {danger:portfolio chart} is not.
callout.label: Old lesson

beats.text: The fixes are boring, and they work. Keep the data verifiable on its own, apart from any view — the chart can be wrong, the numbers must not be. Audit only what's consequential — the portfolio chart, not the poster wall. And notice that disposable cuts both ways: software that's cheap to regrow is also cheap to distrust and replace.

items.title: Keep the data verifiable
items.description: Independent of any view. The chart can be wrong; the numbers must not be.

items.title: Audit only the consequential
items.description: The portfolio chart, yes. The poster wall, no.

items.title: Regenerable = cheap to distrust
items.description: An artifact cheap to throw away is cheap to replace.

beats.text: This was part five of six — and the short version. The full essay has all six views, the precedents from Shirky to Sloan, and the correctness argument in full. Next, the capstone. Part six: what happens when the agent keeps everything it builds — as itself.

slide.title: Software for an **Audience of One**
slide.subtitle: Part 6: {primary:The Workspace Is the Agent} — when the agent keeps what it builds, as itself
